3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Iyer, P., Chu, J., Baker, F. C., Gabriel, K. P., Garber, A. K., Murray, S. B., Bibbins-Domingo, K., & Ganson, K. T. (2021). Contemporary screen time usage among children 9-10-years-old is associated with higher body mass index percentile at 1-year follow-up: A prospective cohort study. Pediatric Obesity, 16(12), e12827.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We analyzed data from the ABCD 3.0 release for the baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10 years) and 1-year follow-up (2017-2019, ages 10-11 years) assessments. Participants with missing data for baseline screen time (N = 63) or BMI at 1-year follow-up (N = 752) were excluded, leaving a total of 11 066 in the cohort.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time (total and by modality: TV/movies, YouTube, video games, texting, video chat, social networking)
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, TV/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social networking
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (weighted average hours/day of weekday and weekend)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time was measured using the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Children answered questions about typical hours per day spent on six different screen modalities... Similar to a previous study investigating screen time exposure from the ABCD study, we calculated the weighted average participants' typical weekday and weekend screen time usage to obtain a typical week measure.

3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Measured height and weight converted to BMI percentiles using CDC growth curves
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: BMI was calculated based on the average of two-to-three measured heights and weights (BMI = weight/height2) by research staff and was converted into sex and age-specific percentiles in accordance with Center for Disease Control and Prevention growth curves and definitions.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multiple linear regression with propensity weights from ACS
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: baseline BMI percentile, race/ethnicity, sex, household income, parent education, site, depression, binge-eating disorder
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multiple linear regression analyses were conducted to estimate the association between baseline screen time (exposure variables, examined in separate models) and BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up (outcome variable), adjusting for baseline BMI percentile only (model 1), as well as baseline BMI percentile, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education and site (model 2)... ABCD's pre-constructed propensity weights were applied based on the American Community Survey from the US Census.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: each additional hour of total screen time per day was prospectively associated with a 0.22 higher BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up (95% CI 0.10-0.34) after adjusting for covariates
Table 2: Social networking B (95% CI) = 0.67 (-0.07-1.41), p = 0.075

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: each additional hour of texting (B = 0.92, 95% CI 0.29-1.55), video chat (B = 0.72, 95% CI 0.09-1.36) and video games (B = 0.42, 95% CI 0.06-0.78) was significantly prospectively associated with higher BMI percentile
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: effect sizes of the associations between screen time and BMI percentile were relatively small for 1-year follow-up; however, cumulative exposure to screen time over decades may become more clinically significant later in the life course
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The primary effect is 0.22 BMI percentile points per hour/day of screen time - a very small effect. The paper acknowledges this but then argues cumulative exposure over decades may matter.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Findings from this study suggest that specific screen time behaviours should be targeted as part of childhood obesity prevention efforts. Guidance for children, parents, clinicians and policymakers regarding appropriate screen time usage is needed to ensure the health and well-being of young people. Health care providers should assess for excess screen time usage and weight changes, especially during the pandemic, and advise about potential risks associated with excessive sedentary screen time.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Understanding the modifiable factors associated with childhood obesity is key in providing tailored education prevention, and interventions for youth and their families.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: Each additional hour of total screen time per day was prospectively associated with a 0.22 higher BMI percentile at 1-year follow-up
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion positions these very small effects (0.22 BMI percentile per hour/day) as warranting clinical intervention. It acknowledges the small effect sizes but overrides this concern by arguing cumulative exposure over decades may matter. It frames screen time as a modifiable factor and recommends clinical assessment and intervention despite the trivial magnitude of association.
